![audio icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Nuvola_apps_arts.svg/20px-Nuvola_apps_arts.svg.png)
![audio icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Nuvola_apps_arts.svg/20px-Nuvola_apps_arts.svg.png)

In [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), the **Earthquake Early Warning** (**EEW**) (緊急地震速報, *Kinkyū Jishin Sokuhō*) is an alert that is issued when an [earthquake](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") is [detected by multiple seismometers](/wiki/Earthquake_early_warning_system "Earthquake early warning system").[[4]](#cite_note-JMA-outline(en)-4) These warnings are primarily issued by the [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") (JMA), with guidance on how to react to them.[[5]](#cite_note-starting-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Introduction

The JMA has two EEW systems: one for the general public and another for the National Meteorological and Hydrological Services.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-JMA-schemes(ja)-8) When a [P-wave](/wiki/P_wave "P wave") is detected from two (or more) of the 4,235 [seismometers](/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer") installed throughout Japan, the JMA analyzes and predicts the approximate location of the earthquake's [epicenter](/wiki/Epicenter "Epicenter"). This allows the JMA to notify people in affected [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan") by TV and radio if a strong earthquake is expected.[[8]](#cite_note-JMA-schemes(ja)-8)

An Earthquake Early Warning is issued to warn the general public when an earthquake of 5 or higher on the [Japan seismic scale](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency_seismic_intensity_scale "Japan Meteorological Agency seismic intensity scale") is expected to areas where the seismic intensity is expected to be 4 or higher.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-aboutEEW-JMA-10) An EEW forecast (緊急地震速報(予報)) is issued to the National Meteorological and Hydrological Services when an earthquake of 3 or higher on the Japan seismic scale (or 3.5 or higher on the [Richter magnitude scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale "Richter magnitude scale")) is expected, or when the [amplitude](/wiki/Amplitude "Amplitude") of P- or [S-waves](/wiki/S_wave "S wave") measures more than 100 [gals](/wiki/Gal_(unit) "Gal (unit)").[[10]](#cite_note-aboutEEW-JMA-10)

The system was developed to minimize earthquake damage and enable people to take shelter or evacuate dangerous areas before the arrival of its strong surface waves. It is used by railways to slow trains and by factories to halt assembly lines before the earthquake hits.[[4]](#cite_note-JMA-outline(en)-4)

The effectiveness of the warning depends on the position of the receiver. After receiving a warning, a person has from a few seconds to a minute or more to take action. Areas near an epicenter may experience strong tremors before a warning is issued.[[11]](#cite_note-JMA-outline(ja)-11)

## Hit rate

The JMA announced the Earthquake Early Warning hit (accuracy) rate for the 2011 [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year#Japan "Fiscal year") on 31 May 2012. The hit rate is the percentage of warnings issued immediately on the detection of [P-waves](/wiki/P_wave "P wave") with a [Japan Meteorological Agency seismic intensity scale](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency_seismic_intensity_scale "Japan Meteorological Agency seismic intensity scale") (震度, *shindo*) number (0 to 7) within one shindo number of the measured earthquake.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

For the fiscal years 2007–2009, the hit rate was 75 percent or higher (75 percent in 2007, 82 percent in 2008 and 76 percent in 2009). The 2010 hit rate fell to 28 percent, due to the number of [aftershocks](/wiki/Aftershock "Aftershock") following the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake (which occurred near the end of the 2010 fiscal year); before the earthquake, the hit rate was 72 percent. Measurement techniques have since been refined to ignore minor earthquakes, and the hit rate for FY2011 increased to 56 percent. The JMA intended to increase the hit rate to over 85 percent by FY2015.

### Inaccuracies

Although EEW (Earthquake Early Warning) accuracy has been increasing, the following erroneous alarms have been issued:

### Improvements

Technical improvements are being made to increase the hit rate, including the Integrated Particle Filter (IPF) and Propagation of Local Undamped Motion (PLUM) methods. The IPF method, introduced on 14 December 2016, obtains seismic source elements with a [particle filter](/wiki/Particle_filter "Particle filter").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) The PLUM method, introduced on 22 March 2018, predicts the seismic intensity directly from observed intensity without estimating the [hypocenter](/wiki/Hypocenter "Hypocenter") and scale. Accurate seismic-intensity forecasts can be made for large earthquakes or those whose hypocenter is unknown.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Broadcast format

### Television

On [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") television channels and other Japanese TV broadcasters ([ISDB](/wiki/ISDB "ISDB"), including [1seg](/wiki/1seg "1seg")),[[22]](#cite_note-22) an alert is a message window on the screen with the earthquake epicenter (shown as a red X with a white outline) and areas affected by strong tremors. Two sets of chimes sound, followed by a voice announcement in Japanese: "This is an Earthquake Early Warning. Please prepare for powerful tremors." (「緊急地震速報です。強い揺れに警戒してください。」, *"Kinkyū Jishin Sokuhō desu. Tsuyoi yure ni keikai shite kudasai."*) In addition to NHK, the announcement is used by [Fuji TV](/wiki/Fuji_TV "Fuji TV"), [TV Asahi](/wiki/TV_Asahi "TV Asahi") and [Tokyo MX](/wiki/Tokyo_MX "Tokyo MX"). [Nippon TV](/wiki/Nippon_TV "Nippon TV") and [TBS](/wiki/Tokyo_Broadcasting_System_Television "Tokyo Broadcasting System Television") shorten it to *"Kinkyū Jishin Sokuhō desu"* ("This is an Earthquake Early Warning"). [TV Tokyo](/wiki/TV_Tokyo "TV Tokyo") sounds a set of chimes, without a voice announcement.

The alerts also inform viewers of possible [landslides](/wiki/Landslide "Landslide") or a [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") caused by the quake in the affected area. If tsunami warnings are issued, the system utilizes [1seg](/wiki/1seg "1seg") to automatically turn on (and tune to NHK) all radios and televisions with [1seg](/wiki/1seg "1seg") technology in at-risk areas. In addition to Japanese, the warnings are broadcast in English, Mandarin, Vietnamese, Korean and Portuguese.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Mobile-phone networks

Japan's three major mobile phone carriers—NTT docomo, [au](/wiki/Au_(mobile_phone_company) "Au (mobile phone company)") ([KDDI](/wiki/KDDI "KDDI") and Okinawa Cellular) and [SoftBank](/wiki/SoftBank_Telecom "SoftBank Telecom") Mobile—have developed [Cell Broadcast](/wiki/Cell_Broadcast "Cell Broadcast") systems to send multiple users an SMS of the EEW.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

It is mandatory for 3G cell phones sold after 2007 to receive this service, although foreign manufacturers such as Nokia, Apple, HTC, LG and Samsung are exempt. In August 2011, Apple announced that its iOS 5 iPhone platform would support EEW notification.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Other

### NTT Docomo Inc.

EEW is enabled by default on all models of the [FOMA](/wiki/FOMA "FOMA") 905i series ([FOMA 905iシリーズ](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/FOMA#905iシリーズ "ja:FOMA")) released after 26 November 2007, and on some FOMA high-speed models in the FOMA 705i series ([FOMA 705iシリーズ](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/FOMA#705iシリーズ "ja:FOMA")) released after February 2008.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### au

The system was enabled on all models in early 2008, including [W61CA](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/W61CA "ja:W61CA"), [W61H](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/W61H "ja:W61H"), [W61K](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/W61K "ja:W61K"), [W61SA](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/W61SA "ja:W61SA"), [W61SH](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/W61SH "ja:W61SH"), [W62SA](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/W62SA "ja:W62SA") and a few [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") models, such as [IS02](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/IS02 "ja:IS02") (TSI01). KDDI and Okinawa Cellular began free EEW broadcasts via au's [SMS](/wiki/Short_Message_Service "Short Message Service"), [C-mail](/w/index.php?title=C-mail&action=edit&redlink=1 "C-mail (page does not exist)") ([ja:Cメール](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/C%E3%83%A1%E3%83%BC%E3%83%AB "ja:Cメール")), on 25 March 2008.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### SoftBank Mobile

On 30 May 2007, SoftBank announced development of an EEW broadcast system similar to NTT docomo's and au's.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Deployment of the system was originally planned for FY2008, but was postponed for two years. On 25 August 2010, EEW service began on [Shikoku](/wiki/Shikoku "Shikoku"), in the [Kansai](/wiki/Kansai_region "Kansai region"), [Tōkai](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkai_region "Tōkai region"), [Tōhoku](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_region "Tōhoku region") (seven prefectures) and [Chūgoku regions](/wiki/Ch%C5%ABgoku_region "Chūgoku region"), and portions of the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region"). The EEW broadcast network has covered the whole country since 7 December 2010. The [SoftBank 831N](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/SoftBank_831N "ja:SoftBank 831N") was the only model supporting EEW in March 2011, although more models had been expected to support the system after summer 2010.

### Other

RC Solution Company developed Yurekuru Call for iPhone, a free [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") application to receive EEW which is distributed on the Apple [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)"); the application is also available for Android. Notification of an EEW might be delayed or blocked if communication lines are congested.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) The Japanese version of [iOS 5](/wiki/IOS_5 "IOS 5") for iPhone has built-in EEW functionality.

### Radio

![Two views of a white plastic radio](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Earthquake_Early_Warning_FM_Radio_EAQ-001.jpg/250px-Earthquake_Early_Warning_FM_Radio_EAQ-001.jpg)

A specific, common [chime](/wiki/Chime_(bell_instrument) "Chime (bell instrument)") tone from [FM](/wiki/Frequency_modulation "Frequency modulation") stations is automatically detected and turns on the radio (if in [sleep mode](/wiki/Sleep_mode "Sleep mode")), sounding a loud chime and broadcasting an EEW message before the quake begins by detecting [S-waves](/wiki/Seismic_wave "Seismic wave"). When the S-wave has been analyzed, detailed information on the earthquake (such as seismic scale and areas under threat) is announced.

The following radios receive EEWs from radio stations and are free of information or connection fees:

EEW broadcasts can be received in areas without [broadband Internet access](/wiki/Broadband_Internet_access "Broadband Internet access"). Signal quality, speed and service area may vary from station to station.

#### Stations

### Cable television

![White receiver attached to coaxial cable](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/KinkyuJisinSokuhoTanmatu.JPG/250px-KinkyuJisinSokuhoTanmatu.JPG)

Japanese cable TV stations offer EEWs. [Japan Cablenet](/w/index.php?title=Japan_Cablenet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Japan Cablenet (page does not exist)") ([ジャパンケーブルネット](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%B8%E3%83%A3%E3%83%91%E3%83%B3%E3%82%B1%E3%83%BC%E3%83%96%E3%83%AB%E3%83%8D%E3%83%83%E3%83%88 "ja:ジャパンケーブルネット")) (JCN) rents a receiver which notifies the user of the estimated [Shindo scale](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency_seismic_intensity_scale "Japan Meteorological Agency seismic intensity scale") and the time remaining (0 to 5 seconds).[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Some cable-TV stations also broadcast EEWs on FM [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") stations and provide free equipment to prefecture and municipal facilities.

### Internet

[Weathernews Inc.](/wiki/Weathernews_Inc. "Weathernews Inc."), a weather-information company, began a paid EEW service ([The Last 10 Seconds](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%A6%E3%82%A7%E3%82%B6%E3%83%BC%E3%83%8B%E3%83%A5%E3%83%BC%E3%82%BA#The_Last_10-Second "ja:ウェザーニューズ")) on October 15, 2007.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) The service requires a computer running [Windows 2000](/wiki/Windows_2000 "Windows 2000") or later with an always-on connection to the Internet. The EEW application can be configured to receive information on earthquakes with a JMA magnitude of 3.5 or higher or with a seismic intensity of 3 or higher. Newer versions of the program allow for the announcement of lower-intensity earthquakes. The program announces the approximate location of the epicenter, the expected JMA seismic intensity and displays a countdown to expected major shaking.

[ANET](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANET "ja:ANET") (アネット), a disaster-prevention technology company which is part of the [Railway Technical Research Institute](/wiki/Railway_Technical_Research_Institute "Railway Technical Research Institute") Group, released an application (EQMessenger) to receive ANET Alert on 7 July 2008. This deciphers and broadcasts EEW information on the epicenter, the estimated seismic intensity at the user's location, and the time remaining before the arrival of the S-wave.[[46]](#cite_note-46) When the estimated seismic intensity exceeds the preset level, EQMessenger can sound a warning and display the epicenter, intensity estimation and the arrival of the tremor on a pop-up map.

A similar, free Windows program, SignalNow Express, was made available by the Strategy Corporation ([ストラテジー株式会社](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%B9%E3%83%88%E3%83%A9%E3%83%86%E3%82%B8%E3%83%BC%E6%A0%AA%E5%BC%8F%E4%BC%9A%E7%A4%BE "ja:ストラテジー株式会社")) after the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

A free multi-platform program, that works on Windows, Mac, and Linux, [JQuake](https://jquake.net), was released on February 14, 2020 as an inspiration to another program called Kiwi Monitor. JQuake tracks information in real-time, and reports any tsunami events that occur.[[48]](#cite_note-48)
A scratch-based program available on the web provides detailed earthquake and tsunami information quickly.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Visual identity

The Earthquake Early Warning logo used by the Japan Meteorological Agency is a *[Namazu](/wiki/Namazu_(Japanese_mythology) "Namazu (Japanese mythology)")* (鯰). Many earthquake-preparedness activities in Japan use the catfish as a mascot; in Japanese mythology earthquakes were caused by a giant catfish, or seeing catfish foretold an earthquake.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## History

The Earthquake Early Warning service began on 1 October 2007.[[5]](#cite_note-starting-5)

After the [2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami"), the EEW system and Japan's [tsunami warning system](/wiki/Tsunami_warning_system "Tsunami warning system") were considered effective. Although the tsunami killed over 10,000 people, it is believed that the casualties would have been much higher without the EEW system.[[51]](#cite_note-51) In April 2011, the [Chilean](/wiki/Chile "Chile") Subsecretary of Telecommunications said that their country hoped to establish a similar early-warning system.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

SUMMARY:
The Earthquake Early Warning (EEW) system sends out notifications to areas expected to experience strong tremors by detecting primary seismic waves (P-waves).

SUMMARY:
Forewarned is forearmed: setting up effective weather, climate, and hydrological services—together constituting 'hydromet' services—and early warning systems (

FULL CONTENT:
![GFDRR logo](/themes/custom/gfdrr/images/GFDRR_Primary Logo_BW-Shade.svg?v=3)
![About us](/sites/default/files/inline-images/main-menu-1.jpg)
![Thematic Areas](/sites/default/files/inline-images/main-menu-2.jpg)
![Partnerships](/sites/default/files/inline-images/main-menu-3.jpg)
![Where We Work](/sites/default/files/inline-images/main-menu-4.jpg)
![Knowledge Hub](/sites/default/files/inline-images/main-menu-5.jpg)
![What's New](/sites/default/files/inline-images/main-menu-6.jpg)

## Learning from Japan’s Experience in Early Warning for Natural Hazards

Every year, natural disasters take a heavy toll on human lives and households, affecting immensely vulnerable communities and economies. Weather and hydrological hazards, such as storms, floods, droughts, heat and cold waves, have been responsible for almost 80% of disasters and over 50% of disaster-related deaths between 1980 and 2011. Forewarned is forearmed: setting up effective weather, climate, and hydrological services—together constituting ‘hydromet’ services—and early warning systems (EWS) allows governments and communities to minimize the damage caused by natural disasters and speed up the recovery and reconstruction.

Japan is a global frontrunner in developing comprehensive hydromet and disaster risk management (DRM) systems. The country has a long history of combatting devastating events like the Hanshin Kobe Earthquake in 1995, or the 2011 Great East Japan Earthquake and Tsunami. Its multi-hazard approach, which covers weather, climate, ocean-related, and terrestrial services, has been applied towards and constantly developed through numerous experiences of disastrous events, enabling the country to accumulate exceptional knowledge and expertise.

“Japan has lessons learnt from challenges faced in modernizing hydromet systems over the last 50 years. Indeed, the key of Japanese experiences is the collaboration among residents, businesses, and government,” said Naoki Yamashita, Deputy Director of the Multilateral Development Banks Division at Japan's Ministry of Finance.

The government, in collaboration with key partners, including the Disaster Risk Management Hub (Tokyo)—managed by the World Bank and the Global Facility for Disaster Reduction and Recovery (GFDRR)—and the Tokyo Development Learning Center (TDLC), recently hosted a knowledge-sharing event on hydromet services with countries from around the world. The “Technical Deep Dive on Hydromet Services for Early Warning” (TDD) is a demand-driven knowledge program with a particular focus on problem-solving to address specific technical issues and client objectives. The TDD, which took place in Tokyo, gathered close to 60 specialists from national meteorological and hydrological services (NMHS) and disaster management agencies from 11 countries (Afghanistan, Bhutan, Ethiopia, Honduras, Lao PDR, Nicaragua, Tonga, Sri Lanka, Uruguay, Vietnam and Zambia), experts and practitioners from Japanese technical agencies, and international partners including the Bureau of Meteorology of Australia and the World Meteorological Organization. The goal of the TDD was to build capacity amongst developing countries to identify the key aspects of modernization for NMHSs, share lessons on challenges and obstacles, learn of Japan’s experience with its modernization processes, and connect with experts and solutions that could contribute to the improvement of the hydromet and DRM practices in their respective countries.

"After learning from Japan's experience and from other countries, we were able to evaluate our work from a new perspective. We found both weaknesses and strengths we had not previously identified and hope to keep working to address our challenges upon our return to Uruguay," stated Gimena Bentos Pereira, one of the TDD participants and Director of Studies and Sanitation Services at the Intendencia de Montevideo in Uruguay.

The TDD served as a platform to share the views on a set of issues pre-identified by participants. As the number of projects that aim to modernize existing hydromet services is growing, the struggle for funding is still critical in many developing countries. Over US$1 billion is committed to investment projects in participating countries, and yet this is far from being enough to address their needs of basic infrastructure, modern observation networks, and enhanced service delivery aggravated at times by conflict or shifts in government priorities.

Very often, the modernization of equipment and infrastructure are not the only obstacles to building sustainable early warning systems. Fragmentation of institutions and lack of clear roles and responsibilities are big challenges for building communication channels between governments and communities, as well as integrating DRM into development agendas. This topic led to rigorous discussions at the TDD, with a focus on key institutional considerations for effective EWS, informed by Japan’s experience.

Dzung Huy Nguyen, a Disaster Risk Management Specialist with the World Bank in Vietnam, underscored this: “Our hydromet services are highly fragmented with no interlinkage – different donors, different systems and technologies, and different institutional setups. The key to addressing this is actually not only about the lack of financial resources, but also the need for institutional reform.”

Most developing countries are facing additional challenges with information and data exchange, and setting up reliable communication channels. Translating hazard information into actionable messages to communities, as well as the role of public and private partnerships, were some of the topics where the experience of countries like Japan and Australia are relevant when building comprehensive monitoring and forecast systems, strengthening legal frameworks and improving impact forecasting.

The TDD also saw release of a new report: "[*Modernization of Japan’s Hydromet Services: A Report on Lessons Learned for Disaster Risk Management*](https://www.gfdrr.org/sites/default/files/publication/DRMHubTokyo_Japan_%20Hydromet_Summary.pdf)," developed by the DRM Hub, which helps build collaboration between the World Bank’s DRM program and the Japanese government, business, academia, and civil society experts. The report provides insight for policy and decision makers that will allow them understand the fundamental operations of hydromet services and consequently target effective funding, in particular to developing countries. In addition, drawing on Japan’s experience in modernizing its NMHSs, it presents lessons learned over a century of changes in legal and institutional arrangements, advances in technology, and responses to major natural disasters. The report concludes with recommendations on how developing countries can undertake modernization processes and projects.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Japan's Earthquake Early Warning System utilizes over 4,235 seismometers to detect P-waves, facilitating timely alerts to the public and infrastructure, minimizing earthquake damage.
Mexico's EEWS, initiated in 1999, sends alerts via cell phones and sirens, relying on seismic data to warn citizens seconds before strong shaking occurs.
The US ShakeAlert system utilizes a network of sensors to detect earthquakes, providing alerts via mobile apps and public notifications, giving users crucial seconds to prepare.
While Japan's system boasts high accuracy, Mexico's alerts are more accessible. The US system aims for widespread implementation, each tailored to local seismic risks and infrastructure.
